title: Quiz: Hybrid Quantum-Classical RL Systems
description: Test your understanding of hybrid architectures combining quantum and classical components for reinforcement learning

questions.question: In a Type A hybrid architecture, what is the role of the classical component?

options.text: It replaces the quantum circuit entirely
It preprocesses high-dimensional inputs before quantum encoding
It only handles the reward signal
It runs in parallel with an identical quantum circuit

questions.explanation: In Type A architectures, the classical network (e.g., CNN) preprocesses high-dimensional observations and compresses them to a size suitable for encoding into the quantum circuit.

questions.question: Why might a hybrid actor-critic use a quantum actor with a classical critic?

options.text: Quantum critics are impossible to implement
Classical critics provide more stable TD targets during training
Quantum actors are faster than classical actors
It reduces the total number of parameters

questions.explanation: Classical critics can provide stable value estimates for computing TD errors and advantages. This stability helps train the quantum policy more reliably.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of using tanh activation at the classical-quantum interface?

options.text: It speeds up quantum circuit execution
It produces bounded values that map well to quantum rotation angles
It eliminates the need for entanglement
It reduces the number of qubits needed

questions.explanation: Tanh outputs values in [-1, 1], which can be directly scaled to rotation angles without saturation. This maintains gradient flow and prevents encoding issues.

questions.question: In quantum DQN, why are separate learning rates used for classical and quantum components?

options.text: Quantum components have no gradients
Classical and quantum gradients can have very different magnitudes
It's a requirement of the DQN algorithm
Quantum learning rates must always be smaller

questions.explanation: Quantum gradients (via parameter shift) and classical gradients (via backpropagation) can differ significantly in magnitude. Separate learning rates allow proper scaling for each component.

questions.question: In hybrid RL systems, state caching can reduce redundant quantum circuit executions for similar states.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. State discretization and caching allow reuse of previously computed action probabilities, reducing quantum circuit calls and improving inference latency.

questions.question: What is a classical fallback policy in deployed hybrid agents?

options.text: A policy that runs when the quantum circuit fails or times out
A policy that always overrides quantum decisions
A policy used only during training
A policy that requires more qubits

questions.explanation: Classical fallback policies provide decisions when quantum processing is unavailable, too slow, or produces low-confidence outputs, ensuring reliable real-time operation.

questions.question: How does gradient clipping help in hybrid RL training?

options.text: It speeds up quantum circuit simulation
It prevents large parameter updates that could destabilize training
It reduces the number of training episodes needed
It eliminates the need for experience replay

questions.explanation: Gradient clipping bounds the magnitude of parameter updates, preventing sudden large changes that could destabilize the policy or value function during training.

questions.question: Which strategies help with real-time deployment of hybrid quantum RL agents? (Select all that apply)

options.text: State discretization and caching
Classical fallback for low-confidence decisions
Blending quantum and classical policy outputs
Latency monitoring and timeout handling

questions.explanation: All strategies help: caching avoids redundant quantum calls, fallbacks ensure reliability, blending improves robustness, and latency monitoring ensures real-time constraints are met.
